videos.title: The GAP Between Rich & Poor in Lima (Peru)
videos.description:
LIMA, PERU. 

Join us as we visit the diverse neighborhoods of Lima, check out a local market, and try some traditional Peruvian food featured in Street Food Latin America.

From our home base in the affluent Miraflores neighborhood, we first head south to Villa El Salvador, a shantytown on the outskirts of Lima — and one of the city’s poorest districts. It is home to around 400,000 people. 

From there, we head to Chorillo, an exclusive, gated beach community right next to Villa El Salvador.

Finally, we check out Surquillo Market #2 to try Huerta Chinen, the famous restaurant featured in Street Food Latin America.

accordingly Lima airport tips flying into Jorge Chavez International Airport Li the main gateway to Lima expect a modern but busy airport experience Upon a arrival you'll go through customs and uber which cost around 15 to 20 USD for a 40-minute ride to Mida Flores or Bano avoid unauthorized taxis at the airport to ensure safety where to stay in Lima for a memorable and vibrant stay base here grab an Uber or a taxi for around 5 to8 USD from Mida Flores or Bano once you arrive take a moment to breathe in the energy of the plaza the grand Government Palace the archbishop's palace and the municipal Palace Stand Tall around you offering a glimpse into Lima's Spanish Colonial past it's the perfect spot to snap some postcard worthy photos while you're here don't miss the chance to step inside the Basilica Cathedral of Lima one of the most iconic religious landmarks in Peru originally built between 1535 and 1649 its ornate facade and intricate interior are absolutely breathtaking inside you'll also find the Crypt of Francisco pado the Spanish conquistador who led the conquest of Peru if you have time opt for a guided tour for 4 USD which offers a deeper look into the cathedral's fascinating history and hidden Chambers from the plaza take a leisurely stroll through the Centro stoico where you'll be surrounded by colorful colonial buildings Lively markets and try a ctio fruit juice 3 USD a mix of various Amazonian fruits Blended together for a refreshing and unexpected flavor also bring small bills as most vendors don't accept cards late afternoon discover Coastal Peruvian food on a foodie tour if you're a food lover one of the absolute best things about visiting Lima is diving into its incredible Coastline Cuisine from the refreshing Tang of Ceviche to the Savory goodness of Lomo Sal Lima is a paradise for your taste buds some must- Tred dishes include Hala Lena fried seafood quas Nas black clams served with lime and spices and tiito a Peruvian take 🇵🇪 10 Surprising Things About Lima, Peru | What You NEED To Know Before Visiting

Thinking about visiting Lima? In this video, we share the 10 most surprising and unexpected things you’ll experience in Peru’s capital. Whether it’s your first time or you’re returning for more, these insights will help you plan the perfect Lima trip. Here’s what happens in the video:

🌤 1. The Weather Isn’t What You Expect

Lima sits on the coast but is often gray and foggy, especially in the mornings.

Blue skies usually appear in the afternoon, but it rarely rains.

The lush coastline is actually man-made—Lima is built on a desert, and the greenery comes from constant city landscaping.

🌎 2. Earthquakes & Tremors Are Common

Lima is on the “Ring of Fire” so tremors are part of daily life.

Earthquakes are rare, but mild tremors are frequent—you might feel chandeliers or glasses shaking.

Locals are used to them, but they can surprise visitors.

🍽️ 3. Lima Is a Foodie Paradise

Lima is considered South America’s foodie capital, featuring Michelin-starred restaurants and incredible local cuisine.

The city is famous for fusion food:

Nikkei (Japanese-Peruvian)

Chifa (Chinese-Peruvian)

Every visit is a culinary adventure, so make sure to try both gourmet and street food.

🌊 4. It’s Not a Classic Beach City

Despite its coastal location, Lima’s ocean is cold and the currents are strong—this isn’t a Caribbean or Mediterranean beach!

Great for surfers (in wetsuits!), but not ideal for swimming or lounging.

Beaches south of Lima are scenic but still have cold water and choppy waves.

🚗 5. Traffic Can Be Intense

Lima is infamous for its heavy traffic, which can make crossing the city take up to 2 hours on weekends.

Plan extra time for airport transfers—you don’t want to miss your flight!

🪧 6. Protests & Strikes Happen Regularly

Demonstrations, strikes, and protests are normal in Lima.

Expect to see police, security, and sometimes military presence.

These events can disrupt transportation and block access to key tourist attractions, especially around Plaza de Armas.

🚌 7. Day Trips Take More Time Than Advertised

Popular “day trips” (like to Huacachina, Ica, Paracas, Islas Ballestas, Nazca) often involve 3–4 hours of travel each way.

While possible in one day, it’s better to plan an overnight stay so you can truly enjoy each destination without exhaustion.

💵 8. Always Carry Some Cash

Credit and debit cards work at most businesses, but street vendors and small shops still prefer cash (soles).

ATMs are widely available, but fees are high and withdrawal limits are low.

It’s smart to bring US dollars and exchange for soles upon arrival.

🚕 9. Taxis Don’t Use Meters

Taxis usually don’t have meters; fares are agreed upon in advance.

Apps are now common and give upfront prices—safer and more transparent for tourists.

🏙 10. Best Neighbourhoods to Stay in Lima

Top neighbourhoods for tourists:

Miraflores: Oceanside boardwalks, gardens, and many restaurants/hotels.

Barranco: Artsy, colourful, bohemian vibe with colonial houses and street art.

San Isidro: Upscale, with luxury hotels, top restaurants, and parks.

Most tourist attractions and amenities are found in these districts.

Bonus Tips:

Explore other parts of Lima, but these neighbourhoods offer the best experience for first-timers.

Always add extra time for travel—Lima is a massive city with unpredictable traffic.

What surprised you the most about Lima?

Share your experiences in the comments! If you found this video helpful, hit LIKE, SUBSCRIBE, and stay tuned for more adventures and travel tips.

We wish we knew about the earthquakes, but we also wish we knew how amazing the restaurants would be and how affordable services like massages and Ubers can be. And we knew Lima would be pretty with colonial architecture. But we did not know about the incredible beauty of the cliffside coastline on the Pacific Ocean, or about all the spacious free parks that are safe day and night, or how remarkably clean and familyfriendly this big city of 11 million residents is. And we wish we knew that we didn't need to bring cash at all. Well, except for one important time. We'll talk about that and go into full detail about what you should know before visiting Lima, the capital of Peru. We're Scott and Maddie with Touchdown Money. We slow travel the world while making money online. First, we wish we knew that one Lima isn't that close. I wish I had known Lima, Peru is far from the US. Our non-stop flight from Miami took about 6 hours in the air. From New York City, a non-stop flight to Lima is about 8 hours, but it's totally worth it. Lima is much more than just a stop on your way to Machu Picchu. I think Lima is worth visiting as a standalone trip. It's that enjoyable of a city to explore. We're traveling the world looking for places we'd consider living in for months at a time while working online. After this trip, we've added Lima to our list. But there is a serious downside we wish we knew about beforehand. Two earthquakes. We didn't know that earthquakes happen somewhat regularly in Lima, but Lima is prepared. We experienced a 6.1 magnitude earthquake. Our building shook and it was a little scary, but nothing fell off the wall and everything in our neighborhood seemed to be intact afterwards. One person did die when they got hit by debris falling off of a building under construction. Three restaurants. We didn't know the quality and variety of the food would be so outstanding. I wish I knew Peruvian food isn't limited to ceviche. We had authentic Peruvian dishes that included the most tender beef ribs we've ever tasted along with soft roasted sweet potatoes. We also loved lomos sautado. Imagine your favorite beef stir fried Chinese food in America. Now make it healthier and imagine the beef is higher quality. Yeah, we didn't realize how Japanese and Chinese food culture has integrated with Peruvian to form delicious fusion cuisine called nikai and chifa. Another mind-blowing Peruvian food experience was duck that tasted like steak. And restaurants in Lima tend to cost about half of what we pay for similar meals in the US. We'll talk about the only time we needed cash in Lima in a moment, but first we wish we knew about four Lima's beautiful cliffside coastline. This was a shock to me. Lima is already a great-looking city, but this breathtaking coastline is a reason to try to book a stay in neighborhoods like Mera Flores or Barono. Lima's coast reminds me of Southern California, similar to like what you find in Laguna or Huntington Beach. Lots of surfers, chill vibes, it's warm during the day and cools off to light jacket weather at night. There's tons of people out with their kids and dogs. In a moment, Maddie will tell you the biggest pleasant surprise of Lima to her. But first, we wish we had known. Five, you rarely need cash. We brought Peruvian paper bills with us, but we were able to use credit cards everywhere we went. And we like that because we use credit card points to travel. The only time we couldn't use a credit card were the couple of times we had to use public toilets in the parks. And even then, there was an app you could download. But instead, we each paid the equivalent of about 30 cents in coins. Maddie, what was your favorite surprise at Lima? Number six, Lima is super clean. I think you brought this up every single day we were in Lima. I love how clean Lima is. We hardly saw any trash or litter on the streets. They even have workers and vehicles who polish the sidewalks. Growing up in New York City, I guess I got accustomed to trash piled up on the streets and general filth. But yeah, it is nice that Lima is neat and tidy. I especially appreciate that in the parks. Which brings us to number seven. Beautiful parks that are safe after dark. We loved going for walks in Lima's parks and even better was sitting down to enjoy a coffee with sweeping views of the Pacific Ocean. There are so many terrific free parks all over the city. Definitely make time to enjoy them. You might even want to book your place within walking distance of a park to relax, play sports, people watch, or dog watch. Every park we visited felt safe day or night. This is part of the reason why we say Liam is so very familyfriendly. The city actually schedules family activities in parks during the night. We didn't see or smell marijuana or any clearly intoxicated people in parks or streets. It's a place where children can feel safe to be kids. And the final thing we wish we knew before visiting Lima is that number eight, services are affordable. Just about anything involving labor was way cheaper in Lima than in the US, and the quality was excellent. We have three specific examples. One, Uber. We paid about $3 for a 10-minute ride in Lima. Two, massages. We each paid about $40 for a 70minute massage. That includes the tip. Three, tipping isn't crazy like in the US. So, we would tip restaurant servers the standard 10% instead of 20% like in the US.
